message.content:
Adversaries may employ non-standard data encoding to obfuscate command and control (C2) traffic, making it harder to detect. This technique involves using custom or modified encoding schemes that deviate from standard protocols. Examples include modified Base64, custom binary schemes, and character substitutions. Malware such as BACKSPACE, Bankshot, Cyclops Blink, InvisiMole, OceanSalt, PowGoop, RDAT, ShadowPad, and Small Sieve have been known to use these methods to encode C2 communications. The impact of this technique is significant as it can effectively hide malicious activities from traditional detection mechanisms, complicating incident response and analysis.

Common exploitation methods include encoding C2 messages within HTTP requests, subdomains, or using custom schemes that transform data before transmission. To mitigate this threat, organizations should implement network traffic analysis tools capable of detecting anomalies in data encoding patterns. Additionally, employing advanced threat detection systems that can identify and decode non-standard encoding schemes, and maintaining up-to-date threat intelligence to recognize new encoding methods, are crucial. Regularly updating security protocols and training staff to recognize signs of encoded C2 traffic can further enhance defense against this technique.
